_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). General Medical: Seizures. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Seizures

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — General Medical: Seizures
points: Prompt cessation of seizures in the prehospital setting
Minimizing adverse events in the treatment of seizures in the prehospital setting
Minimizing seizure recurrence during transport

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Seizure activity upon arrival of prehospital personnel or new/recurrent seizure activity lasting greater than 5 minutes
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Ensure patient safety: clear area of hazards, protect head
Lateral decubitus position (recovery position) after convulsion stops
Oxygen via NRM; suction as needed to maintain airway
Do not restrain convulsing patient; do not insert anything in mouth
Time the seizure duration; document onset and characteristics
BVM if apnea or respiratory failure post-ictal

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose check: treat hypoglycemia as first-line in any AMS/seizure
12-lead ECG acquisition post-ictally
Oral glucose if alert and hypoglycemic
IV access maintenance if existing access
Diazepam rectal (per state/local EMT protocol) for pediatric status epilepticus

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV/IO (adult) or 0.1 mg/kg IV/IO (pediatric, max 4 mg) for active seizure
Midazolam 5-10 mg IM (adult) or 0.2 mg/kg IM/IN (pediatric) if IV unavailable
Diazepam 5-10 mg IV/IO (adult) as alternative benzodiazepine
IV D50W 25 g or D10W 250 mL for seizure from hypoglycemia
IO access if IV unsuccessful
Waveform capnography for airway monitoring

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Midazolam 5-10 mg IM preferred for out-of-hospital status epilepticus (RAMPART trial evidence)
Lorazepam 4 mg IV/IO; repeat 4 mg in 5-10 min if seizure persists
Phenobarbital 10-20 mg/kg IV for refractory status epilepticus
Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV over 15 min for benzodiazepine-refractory status epilepticus
RSI and intubation for status epilepticus with airway compromise
Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV for NMDA-mediated refractory seizures
Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6) 70 mg/kg IV for isoniazid overdose seizures
12-lead ECG interpretation for dysrhythmia in postictal patient
Consider eclampsia in pregnant patient: magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV over 20 min

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Trained personnel should be able to give medication without contacting medical direction, however, more than two doses of benzodiazepines are associated with high-risk of airway compromise a. Use caution, weigh risks/benefits of deferring treatment until hospital, and/or consider consultation with medical direction if patient has received two doses of benzodiazepines by bystanders and/or prehospital clinicians
Hypoglycemic patients who are treated in the field for seizure should be transported to hospital, regardless of whether they return to baseline mental status after treatment

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Many airway/breathing issues in seizing patients can be managed without intubation or placement of an advanced airway. Reserve these measures for patients that fail less invasive maneuvers as noted above
For children with convulsive status epilepticus requiring medication management in the prehospital setting, trained EMS personnel should be allowed to administer medication without medical direction

For new onset seizures or seizures that are refractory to treatment, consider other potential causes including, but not limited to, trauma, stroke, electrolyte abnormality, toxic ingestion, pregnancy with eclampsia, hyperthermia

A variety of safe and efficacious doses for benzodiazepines have been noted in the literature for seizures a. The doses for anticonvulsant treatment noted above are those that are common to the forms and routes of benzodiazepines noted in this guideline
Recent evidence supports the use of midazolam IM as an intervention that is at least as safe and effective as intravenous lorazepam for prehospital seizure cessation

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Actively seizing during transport and time of seizure onset/cessation
' Onset, focality, direction of eye deviation
' Concurrent symptoms of apnea, cyanosis, vomiting, bowel/bladder incontinence, or fever
' Medication amounts/routes given by bystanders or prehospital clinicians
' Neurologic status (GCS, nystagmus, pupil size, focal neurologic deficit, or signs of stroke)
' Blood glucose level

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
